description:
## Goal

Add `eval::Interp` (via the existing REPL) as a parity leg in the fixture matrix, alongside `[jit, interp, aot]`. Drive the REPL by feeding `input.fz` line-by-line — exactly how a user would type it — and compare captured stdout against the same `expected.txt` the other three legs use.

## Why

`eval::Interp` has zero parity coverage today. It is the only execution path that runs without sharing the `FzValue` rep / heap / runtime with JIT and AOT, so divergence bugs in the AST evaluator (or in macro expansion, since macros run through `eval::Interp`) go undetected. Three-path parity (per CLAUDE.md feedback memory) is currently really "three of four"; this closes the fourth.

Originally `fz-ul4.23.9` proposed migrating the REPL onto `ir_interp` to fix this. That ticket is obsoleted: keeping the REPL on `eval::Interp` AND adding it as a parity leg achieves the same correctness goal without the session-state design work.

## Scope boundary

`eval::Interp` has no `Spawn`/`Send`/`Receive` — concurrency fixtures cannot run through it. The REPL leg covers only sequential fixtures, opted in per-fixture via the existing `paths:` frontmatter mechanism. No harness escape hatch.

## DAG

```
.1 (fz repl --script <path>)
        |
        v
.2 (matrix `repl` path)
        |
        v
.3 (opt sequential fixtures into [..., repl])
```

## Invariants

- The REPL `try_eval` code path used by `--script` is the same one the interactive prompt uses. No "script-only" eval branch.
- `--script` mode emits only program-side `print()` output to stdout. No banner, no prompts, no per-input informational text.
- Every fixture with `repl` in `paths:` either passes parity or has a rationale comment in the frontmatter explaining the opt-out. No silent skips.
title: [EPIC] REPL as parity leg in fixture matrix
